title: He Kete Kōrero: The Living Stories of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / He Kete Kōrero: Ngā Kōrero Ora o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a vital waterway flowing through our rohe. This lesson connects to Ngāti Kahungunu traditions of environmental guardianship, the historical significance of this awa as a food source and transportation route, and contemporary efforts to restore its mauri through community-led initiatives.
learningObjectives: Whakamārama i ngā kōrero tuku iho e pā ana ki Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, me te whakamahi i ngā kupu Māori e tika ana (Explain traditional narratives about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using appropriate Māori vocabulary).

Tuhituhi he kōrero poto e whakaatu ana i te hiranga o te awa ki te hapori, me te whakamahi i ngā wetereo Māori (Write a short descriptive text demonstrating the awa's significance to the community, using correct Māori grammar structures).

Whiriwhiri kōrero me te kaikōrero a Hōri Smith (tohunga whakawhānau mai i te awa) mō te tiaki i te taiao, me te whakautu i ngā pātai (Engage in dialogue with local expert Hōri Smith about environmental guardianship and respond appropriately to questions).
Whakatauira i te aroha ki te awa mā te waiata, te haka, te whakairo kupu rānei (Demonstrate connection to the awa through creative expression: waiata, haka, or poetry).

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Kōrero: A Walk and Talk
activities.description: Students will take a guided hīkoi (walk) along a safe section of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. A kaumātua (elder) will share the pūrākau (legend) of the stream's name and point out significant sites, traditional Māori names for flora and fauna, and historical landing points for waka. Students will record new kupu (words) and phrases in their journals.

activities.title: Te Whakarongo me te Kōrero: Listening and Speaking
activities.description: In the classroom, students will listen to a pre-recorded interview (in te reo Māori) with a local kaitiaki (guardian) discussing the health of the awa. Using sentence starters and vocabulary lists, students will work in pairs to formulate and ask their own questions about the awa's past, present, and future, practicing conversational structures.

activities.title: He Mahi Tuhituhi: A Writing Task
activities.description: Using the kupu and knowledge gathered, students will craft a short pānui (notice) or pūrākau (story) from the perspective of the awa itself ('Ko Te Awa e Kōrero Ana'). The task will focus on using descriptive language, correct tense structures, and emotive vocabulary to persuade the reader of the awa's importance and need for protection.

activities.title: He Whakatauira: A Creative Demonstration
activities.description: In small groups, students will create a simple, original waiata (song) or haka (chant) that expresses the mauri (life force) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and the value of kaitiakitanga. They will perform this for the class, explaining their choice of kupu and actions.

resources: Hōri Smith (Local historian and environmental kaitiaki for Ngāti Kahungunu, contact via college office)
'Ngā Kōrero o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku' digital archive on the Mangakōtukutuku College library portal
Field recording equipment (tablets/phones) for the hīkoi and audio for the interview

tasks: Completed journal from the hīkoi with a minimum of 15 new environment-specific kupu Māori and their definitions.
A recorded pair conversation demonstrating the ability to ask and answer at least three questions about the awa using correct sentence structures.
The final written 'Ko Te Awa e Kōrero Ana' text (approx. 150 words).
A group performance of the original waiata or haka.

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Mana Reo (Level 5: Communicate information, ideas, and opinions through increasingly complex and varied texts.)
Te Reo Māori: Language - Mana Reo (Level 5: Understand how the language is organised in different texts and for different purposes.)
Social Sciences: Place and Environment - Understand how people’s management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability.
The Arts: Developing Practical Knowledge - Use a range of conventions in drama, dance, and music to create and perform work.
